md: This trio governs every problem involving motion. The art is in setting up the units and choosing which version to use.

blocks.title: Critical unit conversions
blocks.body:
**km/hr ↔ m/s:**

$1\, \text{km/hr} = \dfrac{1000}{3600} \,\text{m/s} = \dfrac{5}{18}\, \text{m/s}$.

$1\, \text{m/s} = \dfrac{18}{5}\, \text{km/hr} = 3.6 \,\text{km/hr}$.

**Memorize:** multiply by $5/18$ to convert km/hr → m/s. Multiply by $18/5$ for the reverse. This is the #1 cause of wrong answers in time-speed-distance problems.

md: For a single journey at constant speed, average speed = that speed. But when speed *changes*, use:
**Example.** Travel from A to B at 40 km/h and return from B to A at 60 km/h. Average speed for the round trip?
**Right:** Let distance one-way = $d$. Time A→B = $d/40$. Time B→A = $d/60$. Total distance = $2d$. Total time = $d/40 + d/60 = d(3 + 2)/120 = 5d/120 = d/24$.

blocks.title: Harmonic-mean shortcut for equal distances
blocks.body:
When the same **distance** is covered at two different speeds $u$ and $v$:

$\text{Average speed} = \dfrac{2uv}{u + v}$

For $u = 40, v = 60$: $\dfrac{2 \cdot 40 \cdot 60}{100} = 48$ km/h. ✓

This is the **harmonic mean** of the two speeds. Slower than the arithmetic mean — you spend more time at the slower speed, which drags the average down.

md: Two objects move on the same road.
**Opposite directions** (towards each other): relative speed = $v_1 + v_2$.
**Example.** Two cars approach each other from 200 km apart, at 60 and 40 km/h. When do they meet?
Relative speed = $100$ km/h. Time to close 200 km = $200/100 = 2$ hours.

A train has length. Crossing a stationary point (pole, person, signal) takes $\dfrac{\text{train length}}{\text{speed}}$ time. Crossing something with length (platform, bridge, another train) takes $\dfrac{\text{train length} + \text{other length}}{\text{relative speed}}$.
**Crossing a platform of length $L$:** distance = train + platform = $L_\text{train} + L$.
**Crossing another train:** distance = sum of lengths. Use relative speed.
**Example.** A 150 m train at 54 km/h crosses a 250 m platform. Time?

blocks.title: Boats in streams
blocks.body:
A boat's speed in still water is $u$ km/h. A stream flows at $v$ km/h.

**Downstream** (with the stream): effective speed = $u + v$.
**Upstream** (against the stream): effective speed = $u - v$.

Given downstream speed $d$ and upstream speed $u$:
- Speed in still water = $(d + u)/2$.
- Speed of stream = $(d - u)/2$.

md: A boat takes 4 hours downstream and 6 hours upstream to cover the same 36 km.
Find speed of boat in still water and speed of stream.
Faster-by-X km/h. Slower starts T hours earlier. When does fast catch up?
Slower's head-start distance = (slower speed) × T. Fast closes at relative speed = (faster − slower). Catch-up time = head-start / relative speed.
**Example.** A leaves at 9 AM at 50 km/h. B leaves at 10 AM at 70 km/h. When does B catch A?
At 10 AM, A is 50 km ahead. B closes at $70 - 50 = 20$ km/h. Catch-up time: $50/20 = 2.5$ h.
**B catches A at 12:30 PM.** Distance from start: $A$ has gone $50 \times 3.5 = 175$ km; $B$ has gone $70 \times 2.5 = 175$ km ✓.
